# Performance Lab

Measured, not asserted. Every number below came from an actual run of
`benchmarks/scripts/run_benchmarks.py`; raw JSON is in `benchmarks/results/`.

**Test rig:** 2 vCPU, 3.9 GB RAM, Spark 3.5.1 local mode, OpenJDK 11.
**Dataset:** 5,040,000 synthetic holding rows — 600 schemes × 24 months × ~350 holdings — generated by `benchmarks/scripts/generate_scale_data.py`.

The dataset is deliberately **skewed**, because that is the pathology under test. In the real industry a handful of mega caps appear in nearly every scheme, so `company_id` — the join key of the overlap mart — has an extremely heavy head:

```
top-5 key frequencies: 98,287 | 98,198 | 98,176 | 98,143 | 98,118
                       (~35% of all rows land on the top 20 of 2,000 companies)
```

A uniformly random key would make these benchmarks meaningless, because the problem being measured would not exist.

---

## 1. Skewed join — the overlap mart

The pairwise overlap mart self-joins holdings on `company_id`. With the head of the distribution this heavy, one task inherits vastly more work than its peers.

| Variant | Time | Result rows |
|---|---:|---:|
| naive (AQE off, no salting) | 6.62 s | 11,175 |
| **AQE skew join enabled** | **4.34 s** | 11,175 |
| salted ×8 (AQE off) | did not finish inside a 5-minute budget | — |

**Finding: Adaptive Query Execution cut runtime by 34% for free** — one config flag, no code change, identical output.

**Finding: manual salting was a pessimisation at this scale.** Exploding the right side ×8 multiplies the join input by eight, and on 2 cores that fan-out cost dominates the skew relief it buys. Salting only pays once a single hot partition genuinely cannot fit in one task's memory. The instinct to reach for salting first is wrong — measure, then decide.

> Reproduce: `python benchmarks/scripts/run_benchmarks.py --only 1 --schemes 150`

---

## 2. Partition pruning

Silver is partitioned by `disclosure_month`. Same aggregation, with and without a predicate that lets Spark skip partitions entirely.

| Variant | Time | Speed-up |
|---|---:|---:|
| full scan, 24 months | 9.73 s | 1× |
| **pruned to 1 month** | **0.93 s** | **10.5×** |

**Finding: partition pruning is the single highest-leverage optimisation in the pipeline** — an order of magnitude, purely from choosing a partition key that matches the access pattern. Every serving query filters by disclosure month, so this is the right key.

---

## 3. Shuffle partitions

`spark.sql.shuffle.partitions` defaults to 200. Group-by aggregation over the full 5.04M rows, AQE disabled so the setting is actually respected.

| Setting | Time |
|---|---:|
| 8 | 6.20 s |
| **32** | **5.95 s** |
| 200 (default) | 8.16 s |

**Finding: the default was the worst option here — 37% slower than 32.** On a 2-core box, 200 partitions means ~200 tiny tasks whose scheduling overhead exceeds their work. The usual guidance is roughly 2–3× total cores; 32 fits. This is exactly the kind of setting that quietly costs money in production because nobody measures it.

---

## 4. Small files vs. compaction

Streaming ingestion and per-AMC monthly writes naturally produce many small files. Same data, same query, different file layout. Measured on a 251,339-row sample.

| Layout | Files | Time | Speed-up |
|---|---:|---:|---:|
| fragmented | 200 | 1.68 s | 1× |
| **compacted** | **2** | **0.31 s** | **5.4×** |

**Finding: file layout cost 5.4× on identical data.** Every file means a separate task with its own open, metadata read, and scheduling overhead — and at HDFS scale it also consumes namenode memory. This is why Iceberg compaction is a scheduled maintenance job, not an optional nicety.

---

## 5. File formats

Same 251,339-row sample written as Parquet and ORC, then scanned and aggregated.

| Format | Size on disk | Scan + aggregate |
|---|---:|---:|
| **Parquet** | **4.1 MB** | **0.43 s** |
| ORC | 4.3 MB | 0.51 s |

**Finding: close, with Parquet marginally ahead on both** — 5% smaller and 16% faster on a column-pruned aggregate. Not the order-of-magnitude difference that partitioning or compaction delivers. Parquet is the right default here mostly because of ecosystem support (Iceberg, DuckDB, pandas, Spark all read it natively), not because the format is dramatically faster.

> Reproduce: `python benchmarks/scripts/run_benchmarks.py --only 3,4 --sample 0.05`

---

## Ranking the findings

Where the leverage actually is, measured on the same rig:

| Optimisation | Gain |
|---|---:|
| Partition pruning | **10.5×** |
| File compaction | **5.4×** |
| Shuffle-partition tuning | 1.37× |
| AQE skew join | 1.34× |
| Parquet over ORC | 1.16× |
| Manual salting | **negative** |

The two biggest wins are structural — how data is laid out on disk — not configuration flags. That ordering is the single most useful thing in this document.

---

## Honest caveats

- 2 vCPUs is a laptop, not a cluster. Absolute times mean little; the **ratios** are the finding.
- Local mode has no network shuffle, which is where skew hurts most in a real cluster. Expect the AQE win to be larger, not smaller, on distributed hardware.
- The dataset is synthetic. Its skew is modelled on the real distribution, but it is not real AMFI data.
- Benchmark 1's salted variant was stopped at a time budget rather than run to completion. "Slower than 5 minutes" is the honest claim; the exact figure is unmeasured.
- Spark UI screenshots are not captured here (headless environment). Take them when you re-run on your machine — the stage timeline showing one straggler task is the most persuasive single artefact in this whole repo.
